author: California Department of Forestry
title: Fire Safe: Inside and Out
publisher: California Department of Forestry Publication
summary: Defines fire retardant plants as those easily maintained and pruned, drought tolerant in some cases, can be grown without accumulating dead branches, needles or leaves, have a low sap or resin content, and grow close to the ground.

author: Gaidula, Peter
title: Wildland Fuel Management Guidelines for the CA State Park System
publisher: California Department of Parks and Recreation
summary: This reference does not offer a definition of fire retardance with respect to plants, however it offers suggestions of certain plant characteristics to keep in mind when clearing brush to reduce fire hazard: plant vigor, poisonous plants, effects of plants on soils, value for wildlife food and cover, aesthetic values, and relative flammability.

author: Tarbes, J.A.
title: Physical Characteristics of Chamise in Relation to Flammability and Combustibility
publisher: San Francisco State University, CA
summary: Defines fire performance characteristics of chamise species only.

author: Sunset
title: Big Job #1: Landscape to Fight the Fire
publisher: Lane Magazine and Book Company, June
summary: Defines highly flammable plants as those that are accumulators of fuel, not maintained or pruned periodically, and contain high oil, high resin or low moisture in leaves and branches.

author: Sunset
title: Brush Clearing for Fire Safety
publisher: Lane Magazine and Book Company, October
summary: Describes fire retardant plants as those that are low-growing shrubs, non-native brush, and well maintained and watered brush.

author: California State Fire Marshal Journal
title: Landscape for Home Fire Safety
publisher: CSFM Journal, No. 2, March/April
summary: Defines flammable vegetation as plants containing volatile resins, oils, gums and terpenes, and plants that have accumulations of dead twigs and branches on mature live plants. This reference also defines fire retardant plants as those with a high moisture content, high in ash, well irrigated, free of dead matter, and low volume shrubs.

author: City of Los Angeles, Department of Arboreta & Botanic Gardens
title: Green Belts for Brush Fire Protection and Soil Erosion Control in Hillside Residential Areas
publisher: City of Los Angeles, Dept. of Arboreta & Botanic Gardens, Arcadia, CA
summary: The term fire retardant is used to describe plants inherently less flammable than others. Rates plants as HIGH(greatest fire retardance) low-growing succulent plants with thick, fleshy leaves and/or stems. MODERATE(moderate fire retardance) low-growing herbaceous perennials and sub-shrubs not distinctly succulent. LOW(low fire retardance) low-growing shrubs and sub-shrubs with rather dry, leathery or rigid leaves and branches.

author: East Bay Municipal Utility District Water Conservation Division
title: Firescape: Landscaping to Reduce Fire Hazard
publisher: Community Services Department and EBMUD Board of Directors
summary: Defines flammable or hazardous vegetation as any vegetation, including ornamental, that either by it's intrinsic characteristics, placement, or lack of care is easy to ignite, spreads fire rapidly, produces high heat, or creates fires that are difficult to suppress. Defines a fire resistant plant as less likely to burn, grows close to the ground and takes longer to ignite.

author: Brende and Shapiro Tree And Shrub Care
title: List of More and Less Fire Prone Plants
publisher: Brende and Shapiro Tree and Shrub Care, Berkeley, CA
summary: This reference suggests that any plant can be fire-prone if not properly maintained. Arrangement, spacing, density and dryness of the vegetation is probably more crucial than what species are planted.

author: Gilmer, Maureen
title: California Wildfire Landscaping
publisher: Taylor Publishing Company, Dallas, TX
summary: Plants are grouped as the most fire retardant if they retain high levels of moisture in their leaves and stems; these plants are mostly succulents and have low-growth habits. Moderate fire retardance is given to plants that are non-succulent with leaves that retain a high moisture content. Plants with low fire retardance are those with leathery and dry leaves.

author: California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection
title: Fire-Safe Demonstration Garden
publisher: Santa Clara Ranger Unit
summary: A list of plants is offered without definition of fire retardance.

author: Berkeley Horticulture Nursery
title: Fire Resistant Plants
publisher: Berkeley Horticulture Nursery, Berkeley, CA
summary: Defines fire resistant as being able to withstand high temperatures for prolonged periods without igniting and does not readily support open flames.

author: Maire, Richard G.
title: Landscape to Prevent Fire
publisher: University of California Agricultural Extension Service
summary: Fire retardance is not defined in this publication.

author: Northeast Ridge, Southwest Diversified
title: Final Habitat Fire Buffer Program
publisher: Northeast Ridge, Brisbane, CA, Southwest Diversified, INC.
summary: This reference states that all plants will burn under the worst conditions, but some are more suitable for fire-prone areas because of one or more of the following characteristics: high mineral content, high moisture content, low volume of fuel.

author: D'Alcamo, Susan; Rice, Carol L.
title: Appropriate Landscaping Plants to Reduce Fire Hazard
publisher: East Bay Chapter, California Native Plant Society, Berkeley, CA
summary: Supplies a list of California native plants to be used to establish a more fire retardant environment because of one or more of the following characteristics: high mineral content, low fuel volume, high moisture content.

author: Red Shingle & Handsplit Shake Bureau
title: The Green Fireman Plan
publisher: Red Cedar Shingle & Handsplit Shake Bureau, Bellevue, WA
summary: This references bases its recommendations of fire retardance on the following: plants with a high moisture content, low volume shrubs, and (up to a point) plants with a high ash content.

author: Phoenix Team of the Environmental Action Committee of West Marin
title: After the Vision Fire
publisher: Phoenix Team of the Environmental Action Committee of West Marin
summary: This reference provides a list of suggested fire retardant plants; fire retardant is not defined.

author: Morris, Wendy; Barbar, J.R.
title: Design and Siting Guidelines: Bush Fire Protection for Rural Houses
publisher: Country Fire Authority, Victoria
summary: This reference suggests that the degree of fire retardance of a tree or shrub depends on the amount of moisture in the leaves, the amount of oil or resin in the leaves, and/or the amount of dead matter that remains on the tree. Trees and shrubs were rated as follows: HIGH - most suitable; MODERATE - suitable. Groundcovers were rated as follows: HIGH - very succulent, MODERATE - semi-succulent.

author: Brush Fire Safety Committee
title: Make it Safe to Live in the Hills: Fire Resistant Plants
publisher: Brush Fire Safety Committte, Los Angeles, CA
summary: This reference explains that some plants are relatively non-flammable because they are able to withstand high temperatures for prolonged periods without igniting and do not readily support open flames.

author: Radtke, Klaus W.H.
title: A Homeowner's Guide to Fire and Watershed Management at the Chaparral/Urban Interface
publisher: County of Los Angeles, CA
summary: Fire retardance was rated as follows: HIGH - very highly fire retardant. MODERATE - medium fire retardance. LOW - low very retardance.

author: Radtke, Klaus W.H.
title: Living More Safely in the Chaparral/Urban Interface
publisher: U.S. Dept. of Agriculture, Pacific Southwest Forest and Range Experimant Station, Gen.Technical Report PSW-67
summary: Fire retardance was rated as follows: HIGH - very highly fire retardant. MODERATE - medium fire retardance. LOW - low very retardance.

author: Maire, Richard G.; Goodin, J.R.
title: Landscape for Fire Protection
publisher: University of California Agricultural Extension Service
summary: Refers to Los Angeles Arboretum research. Makes clear the point that the term "fire resistant" is used, but that there is not a plant that exists that will not burn given the right conditions. Also suggests that well-maintained and well-watered plants will not burn as readily as those left dry and unmaintained.

author: Grounds Maintenance
title: Flirting With Fire
publisher: Grounds Maintenance, August 23(8):32,36,38,104.
summary: Erosion control was rated as : LOW - 30% or less. MODERATE - 60%. HIGH - 60% or steeper.

author: County of Los Angeles Arboreta & Botanical Gardens
title: Fire Retardant Plants for Hillside Areas
publisher: County of Los Angeles Arboreta and Botanical Gardens, Los Angeles, CA
summary: Bases fire retardance on relatively high moisture content and prostrate or creeping growth characteristics. Rated as follows: HIGH - succulents (90-95% moisture content). MODERATE - non-succulents(80-95% MC) or 70-80% MC. LOW - 60-75% MC.

author: Santa Barbara City Fire Department
title: Firescape Demonstration Garden
publisher: Santa Barbara City Fire Department, Santa Barbara, CA.
summary: This reference offers only a list of suggested fire retardant plants, no criteria are given.

author: Coate, Barrie
title: Water-Conserving Plants and Landscapes for the Bay Area
publisher: East Bay Municipal Utility District
summary: Only offers a list of plants considered to be more fire retardant than most plants. No criteria offered.

author: Rice, Carol
title: Effects of Drought on Landscaping in the Paint Fire
publisher: Wildland Resource Management, Walnut Creek, CA
summary: Lists species with the highest moisture content and therefore probably the most fire retardant. Also lists the driest plants -- these were classified as not being recommended for fire prone areas.

author: City of San Carlos
title: Fire Resistive Plants
publisher: The City of San Carlos, CA
summary: Gives a list of herbaceous perennials, succulents, trees and groundcovers considered to be fire retardant. No definition offered.

author: Beatty, Russell
title: Designing Gardens for Fire Safety
publisher: Department of Landscape Architecture, University of California, Berkeley
summary: Rates plants fire retardance based on the following: Broad-leafed plants tend to be more fire retardant than those with needle-like or very fine leaves; dense compact forms and low prostrate plants are more effective at retarding fire than more open or upright plants.

author: Harlass, Sherry
title: How to Firescape to Reduce the Fire Hazard
publisher: Nursery Manager, December
summary: Defines a fire retardant plant as one that burns slowly.

author: Orinda Fire Protection District
title: Protect Your Home: Landsape For Fire Protection
publisher: Orinda Fire Protection District
summary: Offers a few fire retardant landscape plants with no criteria for classifying them as such.

author: California Department of Forestry
title: Fire Safe, California!
publisher: California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection, Sacramento, CA
summary: Classifies fire retardant plants as those that are hardy succulents and flat ground covering plants that are kept groomed and free of dry leaves.

author: City of Santa Barbara Fire Department
title: City of Santa Barbara Firescapes Demonstration Garden
publisher: City of Santa Barbara Fire Department, Santa Barbara, CA
summary: Defines fire resistant plants as those plants that can regenerate growth, despite burning and fire retardant plants as those which are less flammable than others.

author: Bowker, Mike
title: High Danger this Year: Preventing the Firestorm
publisher: Motorland/CSAA, July/August
summary: Considers that fire retardant plants share the following characteristics: grow close to the ground, have a low sap or resin content, grow without accumulating dead matter, are easily maintained and pruned, and are often drought tolerant.

author: South County Fire
title: Protecting Your Home From a Brush Fire
publisher: South County Fire
summary: Offers only a list of plants, without explanation or definition.

author: Sunset
title: Protecting Your Home Against Brushfire
publisher: Lane Publishing Company
summary: Lists succulents as having the greatest fire retardance. Herbaceous perennials if well-watered and woody groundcovers if well-watered are also highly recommended.

author: Department of Water Resources, The Resources Agency
title: Plants for California Landscapes: A Catalog of Drought Tolerant Plants
publisher: State of California, The Resources Agency, Department of Water Resources, Bulletin 209, September
summary: Offers a list of recommended plants without explanation or definition.

author: LeMay, David B; Wayne G. Mitchell
title: Recommended Low-Fuel Volume Species for San Luis Obispo County
publisher: San Luis Obispo, CA: Central Coast Fire Prevention Association
summary: Rates species of plants as either having HIGH/MODERATE or LOW fire retardance; no explanation or definition of terms.

author: Martin-Richardson, Brenda; San Luis Obispo County Fire Department
title: A Homeowner's Guide to Fire Resistant Plants for the San Luis Obispo Area
publisher: San Luis Obispo, CA: CA Dept. of Forestry & Fire Protection and The San Luis Obispo County Fire Dept.
summary: Lists fire retardant plants for the San Luis Obispo area; no criteria offered.

author: Moritz, Ray; Pavel Svihra
title: Pyrophytic vs. Fire Resistant Plants
publisher: University of California Cooperative Extension HortScript February No. 18
summary: Defines fire resistant as: most broad leaf deciduous trees; leaves tend to be supple, moist and easily crushed; trees tend to be clean, not bushy, and have little deadwood; shrubs are low-growing (<2') with minimal dead material; tall shrubs are clean, not bushy; sap is water-like and typically does not have a strong odor; and plants that will not sustain a flame when ignition is attempted.

author: Moritz, Ray
title: Pyrophytic vs. Fire Resistant Plants
publisher: San Rafael Fire Department, FireSafe Marin. May
summary: Defines fire resistant as: most broad leaf deciduous trees; leaves tend to be supple, moist and easily crushed; trees tend to be clean, not bushy, and have little deadwood; shrubs are low-growing (<2') with minimal dead material; tall shrubs are clean, not bushy; sap is water-like and typically does not have a strong odor; and plants that will not sustain a flame when ignition is attempted.

author: Perry, Bob
title: Trees and Shrubs for Dry California Landscapes
publisher: Land Design Publishing, Claremont, CA
summary: Fire retardance was rated as follows: LOW - high fire hazard species and undesirable domestic plant. MODERATE - acceptable domestic plant that requires ample amounts of water for best performance and valuable watershed species that should be thinned to reduce foliage mass, and be retained in limited numbers to prevent high intensity fires. HIGH - low-growing and high fire retarding plants and low fuel volume native and introduced species.

author: Ellefson, Connie Lockhart; Thomas L. Stephens; Doug Welsh, Ph.D.
title: Xeriscape Gardening
publisher: Macmillian Publishing Company, New York
summary: Offers plant lists only with no definition of drought or drought tolerance.

author: Brenzel, Kathleen Norris (editor)
title: Sunset Western Garden Book
publisher: Menlo Park, CA: Sunset Publishing Corporation, March
summary: This reference was used to gather mature plant characterisitics and information on drought tolerance, climate zones and erosion control. Drought tolerance is defined as requiring little or no dry season water. Climate zones are defined for the western states, each species is listed with a list of zones that it will tolerate. The zone map for this publication was generated from Sunset Western Garden Book's climate zone information. Note is made if a plant is considered to be useful in erosion control, but further explanation is not offered.

author: U.S. Dept. of Agriculture, Soil Conservation Service, Davis, CA
title: Plant Materials Study
publisher: U.S Dept. of Agriculture, Soil Conservation Service, Davis, CA
summary: Offers information on plants suitable for erosion control.

author: Deering, Robert Bowman
title: A Study of Drought Resistant Ornamental Plants
publisher: Davis, CA: University of California, Davis
summary: Offers plant lists only with no definition of drought or drought tolerance.

author: International Erosion Control Association
title: Proceedings of International Erosion Control Association, 8th Conference
publisher: Seattle, WA: The Association. Feb 24-25, 1977, Airport Marina Hotel, Burlingame, CA.
summary: Offers species recommended for erosion control without providing a rating or a definition.

author: Edmuson, George C.
title: Plant Materials Study: A Search for Drought-Tolerant Plant Materials for Erosion Control, Revegetation and Landscaping along California Highways: Final Report
publisher: U.S Dept. of Agriculture, Soil Conservation Service, Davis, CA
summary: Offers plant lists only with no definition of drought or drought tolerance.

author: Resource Management International, Inc.
title: Windbreaks Demonstration Project: Final Report
publisher: Sacramento, CA: The Office of Land Conservation, California Department of Conservation
summary: Offers plant lists only with no definition of drought or drought tolerance.

author: Lenz, Lee W.; Dourley, John
title: California Native Trees And Shrubs
publisher: Rancho Santa Ana Botanic Garden, Claremont, CA
summary: Offers plant lists only with no definition of drought or drought tolerance.

author: Nehrling, Arno; Irene Nehrling
title: Easy Gardening with Drought-Resistant Plants
publisher: New York: Dover Publications, Inc.
summary: Defines drought as less than 1" of rainfall/week or </= 20" for growing season. Plants considered drought tolerant will survive under these conditions.

author: Hazlewood, Walter G.
title: A Handbook of Trees, Shrubs, and Roses
publisher: Sydney, Australia: Angus & Robertson, LTD
summary: Defines drought tolerant plants as those recommended for "hot, dry areas where they will not get any watering."

author: Orange County Fire Department
title: Report of the Wildland/Urban Interface Task Force
publisher: Developed by the Orange County Wildland/Urban Interface Task Force Subcommittee on Fuel Modification. July Attachment C
summary: Defines plants that are not suitable for fire prone areas as possessing some or all of the following characteristics: are known to be especially combustible; have dry or deciduous foliage during part of the year; develop deciduous or shaggy bark; develop dry or dead undergrowth.

author: Perry, Bob
title: Landscape Plants for Western Regions: An Illustrated Guide to Plants for Water Conservation
publisher: Claremont, CA : Land Design Publishing
summary: Categorizes landscape plants based on the water needs. Also provides information on the mature characteristics of plants.

author: Hickman, James C. (editor)
title: The Jepson Manual: Higher Plants of California
publisher: University of California Press, Berkeley and Los Angeles, CA
summary: Provides information on plant classification and mature plant characteristics.

author: The Staff of the Liberty Hyde Bailey Hortorium, Cornell University
title: Hortus Third: A Concise Dictionary of Plants Cultivated in the United States and Canada
publisher: New York, NY: MacMillan Publishing Company, Inc.
summary: Provides detailed information on mature plant characteristics.

author: Costello, L.R.; K.A. Jones
title: Water Use Classification of Landscape Species: A Guide to the Water Needs of Landscape Plants
publisher: Half Moon Bay, CA: University of California Cooperative Extension, San Mateo/San Francisco Counties
summary: Provides recommended watering for over 1200 landscapes plants based on 6 climate regions of California.
